does: This paper provides a comprehensive taxonomy and analysis of machine unlearning techniques that selectively remove specific training data points' influence from trained models. The authors categorize existing solutions into two main classes: exact unlearning approaches that thoroughly remove data influence through retraining or model manipulation, and approximate unlearning approaches that efficiently minimize data influence without complete retraining. The paper systematically reviews these approaches, analyzing their computational costs, privacy guarantees, and practical trade-offs. By examining both categories in depth, the paper identifies key strengths and limitations of current methods, such as exact approaches being computationally expensive while approximate approaches sacrifice some removal guarantees for efficiency. The work establishes unlearning as a distinct problem space within trustworthy machine learning and provides a structured foundation for understanding the landscape of available solutions.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about privacy and security in machine learning that have emerged from research on membership inference attacks, model inversion, and data poisoning. It responds directly to the memorization problem highlighted in recent studies showing that neural networks can retain sensitive training information, connecting to foundational privacy research including differential privacy and federated learning that seek to control information leakage. The paper extends earlier work on machine forgetting and data deletion, synthesizing insights from retraining-based approaches, influence functions for identifying important training points, and gradient-based unlearning methods. It also relates to broader work on model interpretability and robustness, as understanding what data influences model behavior is central to both unlearning and explainability. The taxonomy builds on prior survey work in machine learning but focuses specifically on the newly critical domain of selective data removal from already-trained models.
enables: This roadmap enables future research to develop more efficient unlearning algorithms that satisfy both computational and privacy requirements for real-world deployments. The taxonomy encourages work on hybrid approaches combining exact and approximate methods, as well as investigation of unlearning in modern architectures like large language models and vision transformers where data removal is increasingly urgent. The paper points toward establishing formal definitions and benchmarks for measuring unlearning effectiveness, addressing the current lack of standardized evaluation protocols. It opens directions for unlearning in federated and continual learning settings where models must selectively forget outdated or unauthorized data over time. The framework also enables regulatory compliance, supporting implementation of right-to-be-forgotten legal requirements in GDPR and similar frameworks. Finally, the identified open problems motivate development of provably secure unlearning methods with formal privacy guarantees and practical scalability, positioning machine unlearning as a foundational capability for trustworthy AI systems.
